verses.text: And when the queen of Sheba heard of the fame of Solomon concerning the name of the LORD, she came to prove him with hard questions. And she came to Jerusalem with a very great train, with camels that bare spices, and very much gold, and precious stones: and when she was come to Solomon, she communed with him of all that was in her heart. And Solomon told her all her questions: there was not any thing hid from the king, which he told her not. Guidance: The arrival of the most famous foreign visitor in Solomon's reign. Sheba — most probably in southern Arabia (modern Yemen) — was a wealthy trading kingdom whose merchants controlled the incense and spice routes from the Indian Ocean to the Mediterranean. The queen of Sheba is anonymous in the biblical text; later Ethiopian and Islamic traditions named her variously (Makeda, Bilqis). What matters to the narrator is not her name but her response. The motive: 'She heard of the fame of Solomon concerning the name of the LORD.' Hebrew le-shem YHWH — 'concerning the name of the LORD.' Solomon's fame was not separable from the LORD's fame. The queen's interest was theological as well as political — she had heard that Solomon's wisdom was somehow connected to the God of Israel, and she wanted to verify the claim. The method: 'She came to prove him with hard questions' (chidot — riddles, hard problems, intellectual challenges typical of ancient Near-East wisdom courts). The queen brings the standard wisdom-tradition test: pose difficult questions and see if the famous sage can answer. The journey: a 1,200-mile camel caravan across the Arabian desert, bearing spices, gold, and precious stones as both gifts and tribute. The encounter: 'She communed with him of all that was in her heart' (Hebrew dibberah immo et kol asher hayah im-levavah). The conversation was unrestricted — every question on her mind. Solomon's response: 'There was not any thing hid from the king, which he told her not.' He answered everything. The chapter is setting up the demonstration of Solomon's gift in its peak form. The wisdom granted at Gibeon (1 Kgs 3) and exercised in the two-harlots case (1 Kgs 3) and the kingdom's administration (1 Kgs 4) is now tested by the queen of one of the world's most sophisticated cultures, and it passes the test.

And when the queen of Sheba had seen all Solomon's wisdom, and the house that he had built, And the meat of his table, and the sitting of his servants, and the attendance of his ministers, and their apparel, and his cupbearers, and his ascent by which he went up unto the house of the LORD; there was no more spirit in her. Guidance: The queen's reaction. The narrator lists the items that overwhelmed her: (1) Solomon's wisdom (chochmah) — the answers to her questions. (2) The palace complex he had built — the cedar-and-gold architecture of chapters 6-7. (3) 'The meat of his table' (ma'akhal shulchano) — the elaborate court cuisine. (4) The seating of his servants — the formal hierarchy of court positions. (5) 'The attendance of his ministers' — the protocol of royal service. (6) Their apparel — court uniforms or vestments. (7) The cupbearers — specialized personal attendants. (8) 'His ascent by which he went up unto the house of the LORD' — the ceremonial procession from palace to temple (probably a covered or elevated passageway between the royal compound and the temple platform). The cumulative impression on the queen: 'There was no more spirit in her.' Hebrew lo hayah bah od ruach — 'there was no more breath in her.' The idiom is one of complete astonishment — the queen was speechless, breathless, overwhelmed. The chapter is showing us that what astonished the queen most was not the wealth alone but the wisdom plus the wealth plus the order. The Solomonic court was a working aesthetic of beauty, hierarchy, ceremony, and intellectual depth all integrated. The queen was a sophisticated ruler in her own right; she had seen courts. She had never seen one like this.

And she said to the king, It was a true report that I heard in mine own land of thy acts and of thy wisdom. Howbeit I believed not the words, until I came, and mine eyes had seen it: and, behold, the half was not told me: thy wisdom and prosperity exceedeth the fame which I heard. Guidance: The queen's confession in her own words. She concedes that the rumors she had heard back home were true — but more, the rumors had understated the case. 'I believed not the words, until I came, and mine eyes had seen it.' The queen reports a healthy skepticism about reputational hype; she traveled the 1,200 miles to verify. 'Behold, the half was not told me.' Hebrew lo huggad-li ha-chetzi — 'not half of it was told me.' One of the most quoted lines in this chapter. The queen states that the reality of Solomon's wisdom and prosperity exceeded the inflated reports she had heard. 'Thy wisdom and prosperity exceedeth the fame which I heard.' Note the pairing: wisdom (chochmah) and prosperity (tov — good things, well-being). The two go together; the LORD's gift of wisdom at Gibeon brought both. Jesus would later cite this scene, naming the queen as a witness who would rise in the judgment against the unbelieving generation of His time (Matt 12:42 — 'The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: for she came from the uttermost parts of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and, behold, a greater than Solomon is here'). The chapter's small incident becomes, in Christian memory, a paradigm for how foreigners with open hearts will outshame nominal believers with closed ones.

Happy are thy men, happy are these thy servants, which stand continually before thee, and that hear thy wisdom. Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee, to set thee on the throne of Israel: because the LORD loved Israel for ever, therefore made he thee king, to do judgment and justice. Guidance: The queen's blessing. (1) 'Happy are thy men, happy are these thy servants.' The Hebrew ashrei — 'blessed, happy.' The queen envies Solomon's court servants their ongoing access to his wisdom. The line presupposes the standard ancient understanding that the best place a person could be was in the presence of a wise king learning daily from him. (2) 'Blessed be the LORD thy God.' Hebrew baruch YHWH Elohekha — the foreign queen blesses the LORD by His covenant name. This is striking. The queen of Sheba, a worshiper of foreign gods (most probably the south-Arabian moon god and astral deities), here praises the LORD of Israel by name. Her blessing parallels Hiram's blessing of the LORD back in 1 Kgs 5:7. Both foreign monarchs, encountering Solomon, end up acknowledging the LORD as the source of his blessings. The chapter is showing us the international evangelistic effect of Solomon's reign: foreigners who came to see him left blessing the LORD. (3) Theological insight from the queen: 'The LORD loved Israel for ever, therefore made he thee king.' The queen has grasped the deep logic. Solomon's kingship is not for Solomon's sake or for his immediate descendants; it is the LORD's gift to His people Israel out of His enduring love (ahavat olam — eternal love). The king exists for the people's sake. Solomon was placed on the throne 'to do judgment and justice' (la-asot mishpat u-tzedaqah). The queen names the proper royal purpose: justice and righteousness, in the LORD's name, for the LORD's people. The chapter is teaching us that Solomon's wisdom-and-wealth display had communicated the proper theological truth to this foreign visitor: kingship is service of justice under God, and prosperity is the byproduct of that service. The queen of Sheba is, momentarily, a better theologian than Solomon will turn out to be in chapter 11.

And she gave the king an hundred and twenty talents of gold, and of spices very great store, and precious stones: there came no more such abundance of spices as these which the queen of Sheba gave to king Solomon. And the navy also of Hiram, that brought gold from Ophir, brought in from Ophir great plenty of almug trees, and precious stones. And the king made of the almug trees pillars for the house of the LORD, and for the king's house, harps also and psalteries for singers: there came no such almug trees, nor were seen unto this day. And king Solomon gave unto the queen of Sheba all her desire, whatsoever she asked, beside that which Solomon gave her of his royal bounty. So she turned and went to her own country, she and her servants. Guidance: The mutual gift exchange. (1) The queen's gift: 120 talents of gold (about 4 tons), a vast store of spices, and precious stones. The narrator emphasizes the spices: 'there came no more such abundance of spices as these.' Sheba's spice-trade was the basis of its wealth; the queen brings her country's signature export to the Solomonic court. (2) Almug trees from Ophir. While the spice trade discussion is happening, the narrator pauses to mention that Hiram's Ophir fleet has been bringing in not just gold but also 'almug trees' — possibly red sandalwood from India or southern Arabia. Solomon made temple-pillars and stair-balustrades (the Hebrew may mean either) and musical instruments — harps and psalteries — from the rare wood. The note about 'no such almug trees nor were seen unto this day' is the narrator's editorial parenthesis: the wood was a once-in-history shipment, never repeated. (3) Solomon's gift in return: 'all her desire, whatsoever she asked, beside that which Solomon gave her of his royal bounty.' Two categories of giving: anything she asked for explicitly, plus a separate category of unsolicited royal generosity. Solomon out-gifts his guest. The reciprocity of monarchs requires it; a king who received gifts without exceeding them in his own generosity lost face. Solomon's bounty was greater than the queen's. (4) Her departure: 'she turned and went to her own country, she and her servants.' The chapter brings the famous visit to a close. The queen returns to Sheba with her caravan, having seen Solomon's wisdom and blessed his God. Later Ethiopian tradition would claim that she was carrying Solomon's child and that her descendants founded the Solomonic dynasty of Ethiopia — but the biblical text says nothing of this. The historical record ends with her simple return home.

Now the weight of gold that came to Solomon in one year was six hundred threescore and six talents of gold, Beside that he had of the merchantmen, and of the traffick of the spice merchants, and of all the kings of Arabia, and of the governors of the country. Guidance: The annual gold receipts. 666 talents — approximately 25 tons of gold per year — flowed into Solomon's treasury. The number 666 will become famously charged in Christian apocalyptic literature (Rev 13:18 — 'the number of the beast'). Whether or not John of Patmos consciously associated the beast-number with Solomon's gold-take is debated, but a connection is plausible: 666 marks the height of human royal wealth and the temptation that comes with it. Solomon's annual income from gold alone (not counting his other revenue streams) was greater than the total accumulated wealth of most ancient kingdoms. The chapter adds the supplementary income sources: 'merchantmen' (traders), 'traffick of the spice merchants' (the lucrative incense and spice trade Solomon's geographic position let him tax), 'all the kings of Arabia' (vassal tribute from Arabian client-kings), and 'governors of the country' (the twelve district administrators of 1 Kgs 4 contributing surplus). The chapter is preserving the comprehensive tax base. Solomon's economy combined inland trade, maritime trade (Ophir, Tarshish), tributary income, and internal district-taxation. The numbers underscore the height of Israelite prosperity. The chapter is teaching us, with characteristic Old Testament realism, that 666 talents of gold — by any standard a measure of overwhelming wealth — is also a number that begins to make a reader uneasy. Deut 17:17 had warned that the king must not multiply silver and gold to himself. Solomon, by this point, has multiplied them about as far as it is possible to multiply them.

And king Solomon made two hundred targets of beaten gold: six hundred shekels of gold went to one target. And he made three hundred shields of beaten gold; three pound of gold went to one shield: and the king put them in the house of the forest of Lebanon. Guidance: The gold shields — ceremonial weapons displayed in the House of the Forest of Lebanon (1 Kgs 7:2-5). 200 targets (tzinnah — large body-shields) at 600 shekels of gold each. 300 shields (magen — smaller round shields) at 3 minas of gold each. The total: maybe a half-ton of gold worked into these display weapons. The shields were beaten gold (zahav shachut — hammered gold foil over wooden cores, most likely; pure gold would have been too soft for any use), but the visual effect would have been startling. When Solomon's chariot guard or honor guard paraded through the House of the Forest, sunlight off the gold shields would have created blinding flashes — a deliberate ceremonial spectacle. The chapter is preserving a piece of royal pageantry — gold weaponry that no soldier would actually fight with, but every visiting dignitary would gawk at. Later, when Egypt's Pharaoh Shishak invaded under Rehoboam (1 Kgs 14:25-28), these very shields would be stripped and taken to Egypt. Rehoboam would replace them with bronze copies — a quiet emblem of the decline of the dynasty. But for now, in Solomon's reign, the gold shields flashed in the cedar columns of the Forest hall.

The shields were beaten gold (zahav shachut — hammered gold foil over wooden cores, most likely; pure gold would have been too soft for any use), but the visual effect would have been startling. When Solomon's chariot guard or honor guard paraded through the House of the Forest, sunlight off the gold shields would have created blinding flashes — a deliberate ceremonial spectacle. The chapter is preserving a piece of royal pageantry — gold weaponry that no soldier would actually fight with, but every visiting dignitary would gawk at. Later, when Egypt's Pharaoh Shishak invaded under Rehoboam (1 Kgs 14:25-28), these very shields would be stripped and taken to Egypt. Rehoboam would replace them with bronze copies — a quiet emblem of the decline of the dynasty. But for now, in Solomon's reign, the gold shields flashed in the cedar columns of the Forest hall.

Moreover the king made a great throne of ivory, and overlaid it with the best gold. The throne had six steps, and the top of the throne was round behind: and there were stays on either side on the place of the seat, and two lions stood beside the stays. And twelve lions stood there on the one side and on the other upon the six steps: there was not the like made in any kingdom. Guidance: The throne — the chapter's most evocative artifact. The construction: ivory core, overlaid with the best gold (zahav muphaz — refined gold of the highest quality). A double-precious construction: ivory (rare, expensive in the eastern Mediterranean, imported from Africa or India) clad in the most refined gold available. The shape: six steps leading up to the seat, a rounded back, armrests, two lions flanking the armrests, twelve lions placed on the six steps (presumably six on each side of the staircase, two per step). The throne was therefore the visible center of Solomonic kingship. Anyone approaching the king for an audience would climb six steps past the gauntlet of carved lions — the symbol of royal courage and authority — to face the king flanked by two more lions on the armrests. The number twelve corresponds to the twelve tribes; the lion is the symbol of the tribe of Judah (Gen 49:9 — 'Judah is a lion's whelp') and by extension of the Davidic kingship. The carved lions guarded the throne as the LORD's cherubim guarded the ark — the king sat in a setting that was, by deliberate design, a smaller-scale royal echo of the LORD's throne in the temple. 'There was not the like made in any kingdom.' The narrator's editorial claim is bold but probably accurate; no other ancient near-east throne is described with this precise combination of materials and motifs. The chapter is showing us Solomon at his physical apex — sitting on an ivory-and-gold throne flanked by lion-iconography, the LORD's anointed sitting in unparalleled royal magnificence. Christians have read this throne typologically as a foreshadowing of Christ, the Lion of the tribe of Judah (Rev 5:5), enthroned over all things in greater glory than Solomon ever knew.

And all king Solomon's drinking vessels were of gold, and all the vessels of the house of the forest of Lebanon were of pure gold; none were of silver: it was nothing accounted of in the days of Solomon. For the king had at sea a navy of Tharshish with the navy of Hiram: once in three years came the navy of Tharshish, bringing gold, and silver, ivory, and apes, and peacocks. Guidance: Two complementary notes. (1) The gold service. Every drinking vessel in Solomon's court — cups, goblets, flagons, pitchers — was pure gold. The state dining service of the world's wealthiest king used no silver at all. Silver, the secondary precious metal, was 'nothing accounted of in the days of Solomon' (lo nechshav me'umah — not reckoned for anything). Silver was so abundant in Solomon's economy (see v27) that it had been demoted from precious-metal status. The chapter is showing us a kingdom where the standards of valuable material have inflated past silver entirely. The line 'silver was nothing accounted of' may be the chapter's most economically telling statement. (2) The Tarshish fleet. A second navy, distinct from the Ophir fleet of 1 Kgs 9:26-28. The Tarshish fleet operated on the Mediterranean and possibly westward to Spain (Tartessus, in modern southwestern Spain, is the likely identification of Tarshish — though some scholars argue for Sardinia, Carthage, or other western Mediterranean locations). Every three years, the Tarshish ships returned with five cargoes named: gold, silver, ivory, apes, and peacocks. The list reads like the inventory of an ancient African/Indian Ocean trade — large primates (apes — perhaps baboons or monkeys), exotic birds (peacocks — possibly imports from India), ivory (from African elephants), plus the standard metals. The chapter is recording the maritime scope of Solomon's reach: he had two navies, one east-bound from Ezion-geber to Ophir, one west-bound from Mediterranean ports to Tarshish. Israel had become, briefly, one of the world's major maritime trading powers. The chapter is preserving the apex moment that would not last; subsequent Israelite kings would never replicate this commercial reach.

So king Solomon exceeded all the kings of the earth for riches and for wisdom. And all the earth sought to Solomon, to hear his wisdom, which God had put in his heart. And they brought every man his present, vessels of silver, and vessels of gold, and garments, and armour, and spices, horses, and mules, a rate year by year. Guidance: The pinnacle statement. 'King Solomon exceeded all the kings of the earth for riches and for wisdom.' Hebrew vayyigdal ha-melekh Shelomo mi-kol malkhei ha-aretz — 'King Solomon became greater than all the kings of the earth.' The narrator makes the comparative claim explicit: at this moment, Solomon was the wealthiest and wisest king on earth. 'All the earth sought to Solomon, to hear his wisdom, which God had put in his heart.' The international diplomatic and intellectual traffic flowed toward Jerusalem. Foreign rulers sent embassies; foreign sages came to study; foreign students attached themselves to Solomon's court to learn from his teaching. The tribute-list grew correspondingly: silver, gold, garments, armor, spices, horses, mules — these arrived annually as 'rate year by year' (devar shanah be-shanah — the annual schedule). The chapter is showing us the full bloom of the wisdom Solomon asked for at Gibeon. The young king who had said 'I am but a little child' (1 Kgs 3:7) is now hosting the world's diplomats and sages. The gift granted in private has produced public effects of staggering scale. The chapter is teaching us that genuine spiritual gifts, faithfully exercised, produce reputational and tangible fruit beyond what their recipient asks. Solomon asked for an understanding heart; he received the throne of the wisest and wealthiest king on earth. The principle has theological force: when believers seek what God most wants to give, He gives both that and what was not requested.

And Solomon gathered together chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen, whom he bestowed in the cities for chariots, and with the king at Jerusalem. And the king made silver to be in Jerusalem as stones, and cedars made he to be as the sycomore trees that are in the vale, for abundance. And Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn: the king's merchants received the linen yarn at a price. And a chariot came up and went out of Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver, and an horse for an hundred and fifty: and so for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, did they bring them out by their means. Guidance: The chapter closes with the dark side of the wealth-and-wisdom narrative. The cataloged accumulations are the precise items Deuteronomy 17:14-20 had warned the king never to multiply: horses, weapons, foreign-economic dependence, silver and gold. (1) 'Solomon gathered together chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen.' The military buildup. 1,400 chariots — a massive force by ancient near-east standards. Distributed in 'cities for chariots' (the strategic chariot-bases at Megiddo, Hazor, Gezer, and elsewhere mentioned in 1 Kgs 9:19). Plus 12,000 cavalry. (2) 'The king made silver to be in Jerusalem as stones.' The narrator's striking image: silver had become as common in Jerusalem as the building-stones lying in piles around construction sites. And cedars — the most prestige timber — as common as sycamores, the everyday tree of the Judean lowlands. The economy was awash in materials that elsewhere would be precious. (3) 'Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt.' Deuteronomy 17:16 had specifically and explicitly forbidden the king from causing 'the people to return to Egypt, to the end that he should multiply horses.' Solomon's horse-trade with Egypt is the most direct violation of the Mosaic kingship law on record. The technical details: 600 shekels for a chariot, 150 for a horse. Solomon's merchants imported in bulk and re-exported to Hittite and Aramean kings — Israel had become the regional middleman for horses and chariots. The chapter is preserving the precise specification of how Solomon disobeyed the deuteronomic command: not only multiplying horses, but doing it through Egypt, and turning Israel into the conduit through which the same forbidden weapon-trade flowed northward to other foreign powers. The chapter is teaching us the basic dynamic of compromise: success leads to accumulation, accumulation leads to dependence on foreign economies, dependence leads to drift from the LORD's specific commands. Solomon does not yet worship other gods; chapter 11 will record that final step. But chapter 10 has already shown the structure of compromise that makes the worship-drift possible. The man who built the LORD's house is now violating the law that the LORD's house exists to honor. The chapter ends as a portrait of the Solomon paradox: the wisest and wealthiest king on earth, simultaneously departing from the explicit Mosaic warnings about being too wealthy. The chapter is honest about both halves of the truth.
